en.extract:
The southern hill myna (Gracula indica) is a member of the starling family. It is a resident of the forested regions of south India and Sri Lanka. This species moves around in flocks in the forest, and members of a flock keep in contact with a wide range of vocalizations.


Etymology
The French word for the bird mainate is probably derived from the Indo-Portuguese mainato, from the name of a songbird living near water, and is probably a metaphor since it refers to a man whose job is to wash the clothes of others.


Description
The southern hill myna has a shiny black plumage. It generally measures about twenty centimeters. It can live between 15 and 30 years. The legs are yellow, the beak is bright orange at the base and yellow at the tip. It has white spots on the primary remiges. Like other species of hill myna, the southern hill myna has yellow wattles on the head.


Habitats and behavior
The southern hill myna is present in southwestern India and Sri Lanka. It inhabits tropical and subtropical dry broadleaf forests, mountains, mangrove forests and plantation areas. It is usually found high in the canopy, moving in large noisy groups of about half a dozen, in treetops at the edge of the forest. It hops sideways along the branch. Like most starlings, the hill myna is omnivorous, eating fruit, nectar and insects. 
Its voice is melodious, it is able to emit various croaks and calls. The hill myna is considered to be one of the best talking birds, often imitating human sounds and voice, better than parrots. It is also bred in captivity for this particularity. The southern hill myna is claimed to be less vocally gifted than the common hill myna, a very similar species whose range does not overlap.


Reproduction
It builds a nest in a hole in a tree. They are usually two or three eggs in the nest. Male and female birds look alike. 


Conservation status
Although the population is declining it is considered "Least Concern" by the IUCN.


References

The '''southern hill myna''' ('''''Gracula indica''''') is a member of the [[starling]] [[Family (taxonomy)|family]]. It is a [[Resident (biology)|resident]] of the forested regions of south [[India]] and [[Sri Lanka]]. This species moves around in flocks in the forest, and members of a flock keep in contact with a wide range of vocalizations.

== Etymology ==
The French word for the bird ''mainate'' is probably derived from the [[Indo-Portuguese creoles|Indo-Portuguese]] mainato, from the name of a songbird living near water, and is probably a [[metaphor]] since it refers to a man whose job is to wash the clothes of others.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Chronica de Bisnaga ds Dalg|year=1525}}</ref><!-- where exactly do you find this word in use? A french field guide? -->

== Description ==
The southern hill myna has a shiny black plumage. It generally measures about twenty centimeters. It can live between 15 and 30 years. The legs are yellow, the beak is bright orange at the base and yellow at the tip. It has white spots on the primary [[Flight feather|remiges]]. Like other species of hill myna, the southern hill myna has yellow wattles on the head.

== Habitats and behavior ==
The southern hill myna is present in southwestern [[India]] and [[Sri Lanka]]. It inhabits [[tropical and subtropical dry broadleaf forests]], mountains, [[mangrove]] forests and plantation areas.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Gracula indica|url=https://www.aerien.ch/oiseaux/Gracula_indica.php|website=aerien}}</ref> It is usually found high in the [[Canopy (biology)|canopy]], moving in large noisy groups of about half a dozen, in treetops at the edge of the forest. It hops sideways along the branch. Like most starlings, the hill myna is [[Omnivore|omnivorous]], eating fruit, nectar and insects. 

Its voice is melodious, it is able to emit various croaks and calls.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Gracula indica|url=https://www.xeno-canto.org/explore?query=Gracula+indica|website=xeno-canto}}</ref> The hill myna is considered to be one of the best talking birds, often imitating human sounds and voice, better than [[parrot]]s.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Le Bestiaire cérébral|publisher=CNRS|year=2012}}</ref> It is also bred in [[captivity]] for this particularity. The southern hill myna is claimed to be less vocally gifted than the [[common hill myna]], a very similar species whose range does not overlap.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Gracula indica|url=https://ebird.org/species/sohmyn1?siteLanguage=fr|website=ebird}}</ref>

== Reproduction ==
It builds a nest in a hole in a tree. They are usually two or three eggs in the nest. Male and female birds [[sexual dimorphism|look alike]]. 

== Conservation status ==
Although the population is declining it is considered "[[Least-concern species|Least Concern]]" by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature|IUCN]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Gracula indica|url=https://www.aerien.ch/oiseaux/Gracula_indica.php|website=aerien}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Southern Hill Myna|url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/103878766/135864272|website=iucnredlist}}</ref>
